Home / Space / LUNR
LUNR

Intuitive Machines Just Won $180M NASA Contract — $LUNR Is Building the Lunar Economy

Q1 2026 revenue hit $183.6M (up 194% YoY), backlog exploded to $792M, and a fresh $180.4M NASA CLPS award proves Intuitive Machines is the infrastructure backbone of the new moon economy.

May 26, 2026 6 minutes

$LUNR is no longer just the company that tipped over on the moon. It's the company that's winning everything NASA has to offer — and building the infrastructure of the lunar economy while it's at it.

Intuitive Machines reported Q1 2026 earnings that rewrote the narrative. Revenue hit $183.6 million — up 194% year-over-year from $62.5M in Q1 2025. The backlog? A record $792 million (reportable under ASC 606), with total contract value including options pushing past $1.1 billion. And for the first time in company history, they posted positive adjusted EBITDA.

This isn't a story about space hype anymore. This is a story about a company that's becoming the prime contractor for America's return to the Moon.

The Contract Machine Is Running Hot

The biggest catalyst? A $180.4 million NASA CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) award announced in late April for the IM-5 mission — the largest single CLPS task order Intuitive Machines has received. IM-5 will deliver payloads to the Moon's south pole, including a pair of rovers and resource-prospecting tech that could pave the way for sustained lunar presence.

But the awards keep coming. Intuitive Machines also secured prime contracts to operate NASA and KARI (Korea Aerospace Research Institute) lunar imaging instruments, making them the go-to operator for reconnaissance and mapping payloads on the lunar surface. These aren't small wins — they're foundational infrastructure contracts that establish $LUNR as the default logistics and data services provider for the entire lunar ecosystem.

The SEC filing tells the story: backlog surged from $102.8M at the end of FY2025 to $792.3M at Q1 2026 — a 670% explosion driven by the IM-5 award and other contract wins. When you include unexercised options, the headline number balloons past $1.1 billion. That's not just growth — that's an inflection point.

Q1 2026 Revenue$183.6M
YoY Growth194%
Backlog (ASC 606)$792.3M
Total Contract Value$1.1B+
Adjusted EBITDAPositive (First Ever)
Stock Price$38.26
52-Week Range$7.78 — $38.55
Canaccord Price Target$41
Analyst ConsensusBuy

From IM-2's Hard Landing to IM-3's Redemption Arc

Let's be real about where this company started 2025. The IM-2 mission made headlines for all the wrong reasons — the Athena lander tipped over during its April 2025 landing attempt near the lunar south pole. It was a brutal reminder that landing on the Moon is hard. The stock got absolutely hammered, dropping from the mid-$20s to single digits.

But here's the thing about space companies that actually deliver: they iterate. Intuitive Machines went back to work, fixed the landing sensor issues that contributed to the IM-2 failure, and started stacking wins. The IM-3 mission is now scheduled for H2 2026 — and it's carrying a payload manifest that reads like a who's who of lunar exploration: NASA science instruments, commercial payloads, and technology demonstrations that simply have to work.

The market has noticed. $LUNR has rallied from its $7.78 low to nearly $38.55 — a 395% recovery — as investors realized that one tipped lander doesn't erase a multi-hundred-million-dollar contract pipeline with NASA.

The Analyst Love Is Real

Canaccord Genuity is leading the charge. They raised their price target to $41 after the Q1 print, calling Intuitive Machines "the most direct pure-play on the lunar economy." That's not just analyst speak — it's a recognition that $LUNR is the only publicly traded company with a proven lunar landing vehicle and a direct line to NASA's Artemis budget.

The broader analyst consensus is a solid Buy, with most covering analysts seeing the CLPS pipeline as the key driver. The bull case is simple: NASA has committed billions to its Moon-to-Mars architecture under Artemis, and CLPS is the delivery mechanism. Every time NASA needs to send something to the lunar surface, Intuitive Machines is now on the shortlist — often alone on it.

Wall Street is also watching the Lunar Data Services angle. Operating NASA and KARI imaging instruments isn't just a contract — it's a recurring revenue stream from lunar orbit. Think of it like leasing cell towers on the Moon. The data services business could eventually be higher margin than the lander business itself.

The Bigger Picture: The Lunar Economy Is Real

This is where the story goes from "good space stock" to "generational opportunity." NASA isn't the only customer. The Moon is becoming a legitimate economic zone — and the first-mover advantage is enormous.

Intuitive Machines is building:

  • Lunar communication networks — data relay services for future missions
  • Landing and mobility infrastructure — the Nova-C and Nova-M lander families
  • Resource prospecting — identifying water ice and mineral deposits at the south pole
  • Payload integration and operations — the "FedEx to the Moon" model that NASA keeps buying

The CLPS program alone has a ceiling of $2.6 billion across all task orders through 2028. Intuitive Machines has already captured a massive share, and with each successful mission, their competitive moat gets deeper. NewSpace competitors like Astrobotic and Firefly Aerospace are out there, but nobody else has landed a NASA payload on the Moon and lived to tell about it.

The Risks Worth Watching

Let's keep it a buck — this isn't a risk-free story.

  • Execution risk on IM-3: If IM-3 fails, the stock gets crushed. Period. Every space company lives and dies by its next launch.
  • NASA budget dependence: If Artemis gets delayed or defunded, the entire CLPS pipeline slows. The current administration is pro-space, but budgets change.
  • Valuation: $LUNR trades at a premium to other space names. At $38 with a ~$4B market cap, it's pricing in a lot of future wins. Any miss on guidance will hurt.
  • Competition: SpaceX could theoretically undercut on price with Starship-based landers. Blue Origin's Blue Moon is still in development. The window to establish dominance is finite.

But here's the counter: every risk is known. The market is pricing in IM-3 success and continued CLPS wins. If you believe the Moon is the next trillion-dollar economy — and NASA is writing the checks — $LUNR is the ticket.

The bottom line: Intuitive Machines has transformed from a one-mission-away-from-oblivion story into NASA's go-to lunar logistics partner. With $1.1B in total contract value, 194% revenue growth, a path to profitability, and the IM-3 catalyst on the horizon, $LUNR is the most direct way to invest in humanity's return to the Moon. The risk is real. So is the upside.
